title: HTTP Server Availability Monitoring
dataSources: `index=thousandeyes_metrics sourcetype=cisco:thousandeyes:metric` with `thousandeyes.test.type="http-server"` from `ta_cisco_thousandeyes` (Splunkbase 7719). OTel v2 metric `http.server.request.availability` is a percentage: 100% when the HTTP request completes successfully, 0% when any error occurs (connection failure, timeout, HTTP error status). Attributes: `server.address`, `server.port`, `url.scheme` (http/https), `http.request.method`, `http.response.status_code`, `error.type`.
app: `ta_cisco_thousandeyes` — Cisco ThousandEyes App for Splunk ([Splunkbase 7719](https://splunkbase.splunk.com/app/7719)), Cisco Supported.
spl:
`stream_index` thousandeyes.test.type="http-server"
| stats avg(http.server.request.availability) as avg_avail min(http.server.request.availability) as min_avail by thousandeyes.test.name, server.address, thousandeyes.source.agent.name
| where avg_avail < 100
| sort avg_avail
description: Monitors HTTP Server test availability — the percentage of test rounds where the target web server responds successfully. An availability drop below 100% indicates connection failures, HTTP errors, or timeouts. This is the foundational web application availability metric from ThousandEyes.
value: HTTP Server availability is the most direct measure of "can users reach this web service?" Unlike ping-based monitoring (which only tests ICMP reachability), HTTP Server tests validate the entire application stack: DNS resolution, TCP connection, TLS handshake, HTTP request/response. A server that responds to ping but returns HTTP 503 is down for users — but up for ping monitoring. ThousandEyes HTTP Server tests catch this because they test at the application layer. Testing from multiple geographic agents simultaneously reveals regional availability issues that a single monitoring location would miss.
implementation: HTTP Server tests are configured in ThousandEyes to target web application URLs. Each test issues an HTTP request at regular intervals from selected agents. Results stream via OTel to Splunk.
visualization: (1) Single value: overall HTTP availability across all tests (green ≥ 99.9%, yellow ≥ 99%, red < 99%). (2) Table: tests with availability < 100%, showing agent, server, error type. (3) Timechart: availability per test over 24 hours. (4) Stacked bar: error type distribution (timeout, connection refused, HTTP 5xx, etc.).

references.title: ThousandEyes OTel v2 — HTTP Server metrics

knownFalsePositives:
**Planned maintenance.** HTTP availability drops to 0% during maintenance windows. Correlate with change management schedules.

**Rate limiting or WAF blocking.** If the target web server rate-limits or blocks ThousandEyes agent IPs, tests fail with HTTP 403/429 — appearing as availability failures. Whitelist ThousandEyes agent IP ranges in WAF/rate limiter.

**SSL certificate issues.** Expired or misconfigured SSL certificates cause availability failures. Check `error.type` for TLS-related errors.

**Redirect chains.** HTTP tests following multiple redirects may timeout if the redirect chain is too long or includes slow intermediate servers. Configure appropriate redirect follow limits.
detailedImplementation:
Prerequisites
• All common prerequisites from UC-5.9.1 apply (app installed, OAuth authenticated, HEC configured, Tests Stream — Metrics input enabled).
• **HTTP Server tests configured in ThousandEyes.** Navigate to **Cloud & Enterprise Agents → Test Settings → Add New Test → Web → HTTP Server**. Key settings:
  - **URL:** The full URL to test (e.g., `https://www.example.com`, `https://api.example.com/health`). Use HTTPS targets to test the full TLS handshake.
  - **HTTP Request Method:** GET (default). Use HEAD for lightweight availability checks (no response body — but note this disables throughput measurement for UC-5.9.36).
  - **Agents:** Select Cloud Agents for external monitoring ("can internet users reach us?") and Enterprise Agents for internal monitoring ("can employees reach internal apps?"). Use agents in all regions where you have users.
  - **Interval:** 1 minute for critical production endpoints, 5 minutes for less critical.
  - **Redirect Follow:** Configure maximum redirect depth (default: 5). Disable if you want to test a specific URL without following redirects.
  - **SSL Verification:** Enable SSL verification to detect certificate issues (expiry, mismatch, revocation).
• **ThousandEyes agent IPs whitelisted.** If the target has a WAF, firewall, or rate limiter, whitelist ThousandEyes agent IP ranges. ThousandEyes publishes agent IP ranges at `https://app.thousandeyes.com/sfdc/ip-addresses`. Failure to whitelist causes false availability failures.
• **Splunk role:** `srchIndexesAllowed` must include `thousandeyes_metrics`.

Step 1 — Configure data collection
HTTP Server test metrics flow through the same Tests Stream — Metrics OTel input configured in UC-5.9.1. No separate input is needed.

Verify HTTP test data is present:
```spl
index=thousandeyes_metrics sourcetype="cisco:thousandeyes:metric" thousandeyes.test.type="http-server" earliest=-30m
| stats count dc(thousandeyes.source.agent.name) as agents by thousandeyes.test.name, server.address
| sort thousandeyes.test.name
```
Each test should show data from all assigned agents. If some agents are missing, check agent health in ThousandEyes → **Cloud & Enterprise Agents → Agent Settings**.

**Understanding HTTP Server metrics in OTel v2:**
- `http.server.request.availability` (percentage: 0 or 100) — 100% if the HTTP request completed successfully (connection established, response received with expected status code). 0% if ANY error occurred: connection refused, timeout, TLS error, HTTP 5xx error, or response content mismatch.
- `http.client.request.duration` (seconds) — Time to First Byte (TTFB): time from sending the HTTP request to receiving the first byte of the response. See UC-5.9.35 for detailed monitoring.
- `http.server.throughput` (bytes/second) — download throughput for the response body. See UC-5.9.36 for detailed monitoring.
- `http.response.status_code` — HTTP status code (200, 301, 403, 500, etc.). Only present when the server responds.
- `error.type` — error category when availability = 0% (e.g., `timeout`, `connection_refused`, `ssl_error`, `dns_resolution_error`).

Step 2 — Create the search and alert
**HTTP availability overview (flags any test with failures):**
```spl
`stream_index` thousandeyes.test.type="http-server"
| stats avg(http.server.request.availability) as avg_avail min(http.server.request.availability) as min_avail by thousandeyes.test.name, server.address, thousandeyes.source.agent.name
| where avg_avail < 100
| sort avg_avail
```

**Understanding this SPL**

`thousandeyes.test.type="http-server"` — filters to HTTP Server tests. Other test types (page-load, web-transactions, api) measure web performance but test different aspects.

`avg(http.server.request.availability)` — the average availability over the search window. 100% means every test round succeeded. Values between 0% and 100% indicate intermittent failures. 0% means every round failed.

`min(http.server.request.availability)` — the worst single reading. If `avg_avail` is 95% but `min_avail` is 0%, some rounds completely failed (total outage) while most succeeded (intermittent issue).

`where avg_avail < 100` — ANY availability failure is significant for HTTP endpoints. Unlike latency (where minor variations are acceptable), HTTP availability should be 100%. A single failed round means a user experienced a broken page.

**Error analysis (identifies WHY tests are failing):**
```spl
`stream_index` thousandeyes.test.type="http-server" http.server.request.availability=0
| stats count by thousandeyes.test.name, error.type, http.response.status_code
| sort -count
```
This shows the error distribution for failed rounds. Common patterns:
- `error.type="timeout"` → server is overloaded or network path is broken.
- `error.type="connection_refused"` → server process is down (port not listening).
- `error.type="ssl_error"` → TLS certificate issue (expired, mismatch, revoked).
- `error.type="dns_resolution_error"` → DNS not resolving (check UC-5.9.13).
- `http.response.status_code=503` → application returning Service Unavailable.
- `http.response.status_code=403` → firewall or WAF blocking the agent.

**Availability timechart (visual timeline of outages):**
```spl
`stream_index` thousandeyes.test.type="http-server" earliest=-24h
| timechart span=5m avg(http.server.request.availability) by thousandeyes.test.name
```
Use `span=5m` for granular outage detection (with 1-minute test intervals, each 5-minute bin has ~5 data points per agent).

**Aggregate availability per test (fleet-level view):**
```spl
`stream_index` thousandeyes.test.type="http-server" earliest=-24h
| stats avg(http.server.request.availability) as availability dc(thousandeyes.source.agent.name) as agents by thousandeyes.test.name, server.address
| eval sla_met=if(availability >= 99.9, "Yes", "No")
| sort availability
```

**Scheduling:** cron `*/5 * * * *`, time range `-10m to now`. HTTP availability failures need fast alerting — a 5-minute detection window means you find out about a failure within 10 minutes of it starting. For critical endpoints (e-commerce checkout, authentication), consider `*/2 * * * *` with `-5m to now`. Throttle by `thousandeyes.test.name` + `server.address` for 1 hour.

Step 3 — Validate
(a) **Manual browser test.** Open the test URL in your browser. If the site loads but ThousandEyes shows 0% availability, check for: (i) IP-based restrictions (WAF, firewall, geo-blocking) that block ThousandEyes agent IPs. (ii) Expected response content — if the test validates response body content ("Verify Content" setting in ThousandEyes), a changed page may fail validation.

(b) **Cross-reference ThousandEyes UI.** Navigate to **Cloud & Enterprise Agents → Views → HTTP Server** and select the same test and time window. The UI shows per-agent availability as color-coded dots (green = success, red = failure). Compare with Splunk data.

(c) **Verify error field population.** Run `| fieldsummary | search field=error.type OR field=http.response.status_code`. If these fields are absent, the data may be v1 format (different field names). Check `thousandeyes.data.version` — v1 uses different field names for error categorization.

(d) **Test from multiple agents.** If only one agent shows failures, the issue may be agent-specific (local network, DNS resolver, or firewall). If all agents show failures, the issue is on the server side.

(e) **SSL certificate validation.** If `error.type="ssl_error"`, check the certificate: `openssl s_client -connect <server.address>:443 -servername <hostname> < /dev/null 2>&1 | openssl x509 -noout -dates`. Verify the certificate is valid, not expired, and matches the hostname.

(f) **HTTP status code distribution.** `| stats count by http.response.status_code | sort -count`. Healthy tests should show primarily 200. Redirects (301/302) should be followed by the test (check redirect settings). 4xx/5xx indicate application or access issues.

Step 4 — Operationalize
**Dashboard** ("Web Application Availability" — designed for SRE team / NOC wall display):
• Row 1 — Single value tiles: "Endpoints 100% available" (green), "Endpoints with failures" (red count), "Overall fleet availability %" (green ≥ 99.9%, yellow ≥ 99%, red < 99%). Make these large and prominent — this is the "everything works" indicator.
• Row 2 — Availability timechart over 24 hours at 5-minute granularity. Each line is a test. Red areas indicate outage periods. Use area chart with stacked fills for clear outage visualization.
• Row 3 — Error analysis: stacked bar chart of error types (timeout, connection_refused, ssl_error, DNS error, HTTP 5xx). Shows the dominant failure mode.
• Row 4 — Detail table: test name | server address | agent | avg availability | min availability | error type | HTTP status — sorted worst-first. Drilldown to ThousandEyes permalink.

**Alerting:**
• Availability < 100% for ANY production endpoint → immediate page (PagerDuty high-urgency). HTTP outages have direct user impact. Include: test name, server address, affected agents, error type, and ThousandEyes permalink.
• Availability < 100% from ALL agents → critical incident. All-hands page. The endpoint is globally unreachable.
• Availability < 100% from specific agents only → low-urgency notification. Regional issue — investigate the network path from affected agents.

**Runbook** (owner: application SRE / on-call):
1. **Availability 0% from all agents.** Server is down. Check: (a) Application process status (systemctl, docker ps). (b) Load balancer health checks — are all backend instances unhealthy? (c) DNS resolution — is the domain still resolving? (UC-5.9.13). (d) Server network connectivity — can the server reach external services?
2. **Availability 0% from some agents only.** Regional issue. Check: (a) CDN edge node health for that region. (b) DNS resolution differences between regions (Anycast routing). (c) ISP reachability — an Internet Insights event may explain it (UC-5.9.18).
3. **`error.type="timeout"`** Server is overloaded or network path is broken. Check: (a) Server CPU/memory utilization. (b) Application request queue depth. (c) Network latency to the server (UC-5.9.1). (d) Firewall connection table limits.
4. **`error.type="ssl_error"`** TLS certificate issue. Check: (a) Certificate expiry date. (b) Certificate hostname match. (c) Certificate chain completeness (missing intermediate CA). (d) TLS protocol version compatibility.
5. **`http.response.status_code=503`** Application returning Service Unavailable. Check: (a) Backend health checks. (b) Application deployment status (is a deployment in progress?). (c) Database connectivity from the application.
6. **`http.response.status_code=403`** Access denied. Check: (a) WAF rules — are ThousandEyes agent IPs whitelisted? (b) Geo-blocking — is the agent in a blocked region? (c) Rate limiting — has the test frequency exceeded the rate limit?
7. **Intermittent failures (avg_avail between 50–99%).** Flapping server or load-balanced backend with one unhealthy instance. Check load balancer logs for which backend is failing.

Step 5 — Troubleshooting

• **Always 0% availability despite server being up** — Most common cause: ThousandEyes agent IPs are blocked by WAF, firewall, or geo-blocking. Whitelist ThousandEyes agent IP ranges. Check with: `curl -H 'X-Forwarded-For: <agent_ip>' https://<target>/` to simulate traffic from the agent IP.

• **Availability drops briefly then recovers** — The server may be doing rolling restarts during deployment. Check deployment schedules. Consider adding a maintenance window filter to the search.

• **`error.type` field empty but availability = 0%** — In OTel v1 data, the error classification field may be named differently. Check `| fieldsummary | search field=error*` to find the v1 equivalent.

• **HTTP 301/302 responses showing as failures** — The test may not be following redirects. Check the test configuration in ThousandEyes → test settings → "Follow Redirects" option. If disabled, a redirect response is treated as a failure.

• **Tests passing in ThousandEyes UI but showing failures in Splunk** — Timing difference. The Splunk data may include a test round that the UI's default view filters out. Check the exact time range.

• **All common troubleshooting** — See UC-5.9.1 Step 5 for HEC connectivity, OAuth refresh, macro configuration, v1/v2 field name differences, and role permissions.

**IPv6 Note:** ICMPv6 is architecturally critical for IPv6 — it carries NDP (Neighbor Discovery), Path MTU Discovery, and Multicast Listener Discovery. Unlike ICMP for IPv4, blocking ICMPv6 breaks IPv6 connectivity entirely. Ensure firewall policies permit at minimum ICMPv6 types 1-4 (Destination Unreachable, Packet Too Big, Time Exceeded, Parameter Problem) and types 133-137 (RS, RA, NS, NA, Redirect). See RFC 4890 for filtering recommendations.
grandmaExplanation: We check that our website actually works by testing it from many places around the world every few minutes — because a website that works from our office might be broken for customers in other countries.
